perturbation.original_text: Philips invoices, compliant with the requirements of this Agreement, will be payable within [Redacted - Commercially Sensitive] following the end of the month of the date of invoice. Customer shall make all payments in Euros by check, wire transfer or automated clearing house to the bank account designated by Philips.
perturbation.changed_text: Philips invoices, compliant with the requirements of this Agreement, will be payable within 120 days following the end of the month of the date of invoice. Customer shall make all payments in Euros by check, wire transfer or automated clearing house to the bank account designated by Philips.
perturbation.explanation: Extended payment terms beyond standard practices can conflict with prompt payment laws or regulations designed to protect suppliers, especially if the original agreement had shorter payment terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Directive 2011/7/EU of the European Parliament and of the Council of 16 February 2011 on combating late payment in commercial transactions
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of Directive 2011/7/EU generally limits payment terms to 60 calendar days unless expressly agreed otherwise and not grossly unfair to the creditor. A 120-day payment term could be seen as violating this directive, especially if the supplier is a smaller entity and lacks bargaining power.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: y not compel state governors to surrender fugitives through the issue of writs of mandamus . The Dennison decision was overruled by Puerto Rico v. Branstad (1987); now, the federal courts may require the extradition of fugitives. [ 2 ] Alleged fugitives generally may not challenge extradition proceedings. The motives of the governor demanding the extradition may not be questioned. The accused cannot defend himself against the charges in the extraditing state; the fugitive must do so in the state receiving him. However, the accused may prevent extradition by offering clear evidence that he was not in the state he allegedly fled from at the time of the crime. [ 3 ] There is no constitutional requirement that extradited fugitives be tried only for the crimes named in the extradition proceedings. Fugitives brought to states by means other than extradition may be tried, even though the means of the conveyance was unlawful; the Supreme Court so ruled in Mahon v. Justice , 127 U.S. 700 (1888). In Mahon a body of armed men from Kentucky forcibly took, without a warrant, a man in West Virginia to bring him back to Kentucky for formal arrest and trial. Clause 3: Fugitive Slave Clause [ edit ] Main article: Fugitive Slave Clause No Person held to Service or Labour in one State, under the Laws thereof, escaping into another, shall, in Consequence of any Law or Regulation therein, be discharged from such Service or Labour, but shall be delivered up on Claim of the Party to whom such Service or Labour may be due. Pierce Butler and Charles Pinckney , both from South Carolina , submitted this clause to the Constitutional Convention . James Wilson of Pennsylvania objected, stating it would require that state governments enforce slavery at taxpayers' expense. Butler withdrew the clause. However, on the next day the clause was quietly reinstated and adopted by the Convention without objection. This clause was added to the clause that provided extradition for fugitives from justice. [ 4 ] When first adopted, this clause applied to fugitive slaves and required that they be extradited upon the claims of their masters, but it provided no means for doing so. The Fugitive Slave Act of 1793 created the mechanism for recovering a fugitive slave, overruled any state laws giving sanctuary , made it a federal crime to assist an escaped slave, and allowed slave-catchers into every U.S. state and territory. As free states sought to undermine the federal law, the even more severe Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 was enacted. In 1864, during the Civil War, an effort to repeal this clause of the Constitution failed. The vote in the House was 69 for repeal and 38 against, which was short of the two-to-one vote required to amend the Constitution. [ 5 ] This clause was rendered mostly moot when the Thirteenth Amendment abolished involuntary servitude . Section 3: New states and federal property [ edit ] Clause 1: Admission of new states [ edit ] Main article: Admission to the Union See also: List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union New States may be admitted by the Congress into this Union; but no new States shall be formed or erected within the Jurisdiction of any other State; nor any State be formed by the Junction of two or more States, or parts of States, without the Consent of the Legislatures of the States concerned as well as of the Congress. The First Clause of Section Three, also known as the Admissions Clause , [ 6 ] grants to Congress the authority to admit new states into the Union. Since the establishment of the United States in 1776, the number of states has expanded from the original 13 to 50. It also forbids the creation of new states from parts of existing states without the consent of the affected states legislature and Congress. This latter provision was designed to give Eastern states that still had claims to Western lands (e.g., Virginia and North Carolina ) to have a veto over whether their western counties (which eventually became Kent
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment terms to 120 days, which the Law Explanation suggests may violate Article 4 of Directive 2011/7/EU. This directive generally limits payment terms to 60 days unless expressly agreed otherwise and not grossly unfair to the creditor. The Perturbation Explanation also mentions that extended payment terms can conflict with prompt payment laws. Although the provided law snippets don't directly address this directive or prompt payment laws, the Law Explanation suggests a legal contradiction by indicating that a 120-day term could violate payment term limits under EU law. Therefore, the change introduces a potentially unenforceable term.

perturbation.original_text: Each party's obligation to protect the Confidential Information of the other Party shall continue for a period of [Redacted - Commercial Sensitive - Time Period] following the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party's obligation to protect the Confidential Information of the other Party shall cease immediately upon the date of termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Eliminating confidentiality obligations upon termination contradicts standard practices and legal principles surrounding trade secret protection, potentially violating laws designed to safeguard proprietary information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) protects trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. Ceasing confidentiality obligations upon termination would eliminate protection, contradicting the UTSA's aim to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. Even though the UTSA is a state law, it is very similar across states.

perturbation.justification: The original contract ensures confidentiality post-termination, aligning with trade secret protection principles as per the LLM's explanation concerning the UTSA. The changed text explicitly eliminates this post-termination confidentiality, directly contradicting the intention to protect trade secrets even after the agreement ends. While no specific statute is included in the scraped snippets to demonstrate the explicit violation, the LLM explanation indicates that this change undermines the legal principles that protect trade secrets, suggesting this change would fail legal scrutiny. The perturbation explanation also emphasizes how the changed text goes against standard practices.

perturbation.original_text: Customer, in its sole discretion, may terminate this Agreement, without cause, by providing six (6) months prior written notice to Philips. Philips may terminate this Agreement with immediate effect by written notice to Customer, should Customer not have filed with the specified bodies to transfer the applicable registrations within the time period specified in clause 2.3.
perturbation.changed_text: Customer, in its sole discretion, may terminate this Agreement, without cause, by providing thirty (30) days prior written notice to Philips. Philips may terminate this Agreement with immediate effect by written notice to Customer, should Customer not have filed with the specified bodies to transfer the applicable registrations within the time period specified in clause 2.3.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination without cause may violate the principle of good faith and fair dealing, especially if Philips relies on the agreement and needs adequate time to adjust its operations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6:248 of the Netherlands Civil Code states that a contract has not only the legal consequences which parties have agreed upon, but also those which, according to the nature of the contract, result from law, usage, or the requirements of reasonableness and fairness. Reducing a termination notice to 30 days could be seen as violating the requirement of reasonableness and fairness if it causes undue hardship to Philips, particularly given the potential reliance and investment related to manufacturing the products. Netherlands law is governing this contract in accordance to section 18.8

perturbation.justification: The original text requires a six-month notice period for termination without cause, while the changed text reduces this to thirty days. The Law Explanation references Article 6:248 of the Netherlands Civil Code, stating that contracts must adhere to reasonableness and fairness. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that reducing the notice period could violate the principle of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the structure of the Burgerlijk Wetboek, giving context to Article 6:248, and the modified contract conflicts with the principle of reasonableness and fairness embedded in that law, making the changed text a contradiction. Therefore, based on Article 6:248, the edited version is a contradiction.
